text: The laparoscopic view shows the cecum and appendix adherent to the right parietal wall. The appendix is visible with a healthy base, while the peritoneum over the appendix is being meticulously coagulated and cut.

The surgeon carefully strips the peritoneum away from the appendix, tracing its course from the base towards the tip. The mid part of the appendix appears distended, potentially indicating the presence of a fecolith or abscess.

The surgeon identifies the tip of the appendix in a subhepatic position and performs a retrograde appendectomy. The mesoappendix near the base is divided using bipolar cautery, with suction used to maintain a clear field. The appendix is then divided away from the cecum to clearly define its base.

Saline lavage is applied to clear the surgical field of blood, enhancing visibility. The surgeon divides the mesoappendix, revealing the site of perforation with a small abscess. The mucosa of the appendix is visible.

The surgeon performs a retrograde appendectomy, progressively stripping the mucosa from the muscle and dissecting the serosa up to the tip of the appendix. Using gentle traction and energy application, the appendix is separated from the cecum and ascending colon, aided by irrigation and saline lavage.

The surgeon releases the attachments at the base of the appendix, revealing a 1.5 cm stump. A catgut loop is applied with two knots close to the base, and the remaining stump is excised. The specimen is extracted through the titanium port, followed by thorough saline lavage of the surgical field.
